title: Composting without a garden
subtitle: Bokashi, worm bins, and council collection for flats and small spaces
excerpt: A lack of outdoor space does not prevent diverting kitchen waste from landfill. A worm bin processes fruit and vegetable peelings in a flat. A bokashi system handles meat, dairy, and cooked food that a worm bin cannot. Council food waste collection covers anything else.
sourceNotes: Garden Organic worm composting guidance. Bokashi UK technical notes on anaerobic fermentation of food waste. WRAP household food waste diversion data for UK local authorities.

glossaryTerms.definition: An anaerobic fermentation method for food waste using bran inoculated with effective microorganisms (EM). Bokashi does not produce compost directly; it produces a pre-fermented material that must be buried in soil or added to a compost heap to finish decomposition. It accepts meat, fish, dairy, and cooked food that a conventional heap cannot.

content.text: The average UK household produces around 70 kg of avoidable food waste per year, of which roughly half is fruit and vegetable matter. For households without outdoor space, three routes exist: a worm bin for raw fruit and vegetable scraps, a bokashi system for cooked food, meat, and dairy, and local authority food waste collection for everything else. All three work in a flat or a home with no garden.

A worm bin uses composting worms (Eisenia fetida, also called red wigglers or tiger worms) to convert raw fruit and vegetable scraps into worm castings and liquid worm feed. A correctly managed bin does not smell and can be kept in a kitchen, under a sink, or in a shaded outdoor space down to about 5 degrees C. The liquid from the sump can be diluted 1:10 and used as a liquid feed on houseplants or a balcony container garden.

Feed: raw fruit and vegetable scraps, coffee grounds, tea bags (without plastic mesh), crushed eggshells, torn cardboard. Add roughly equal volumes of scraps and torn cardboard to keep the bin from becoming too wet.
Do not add: meat, fish, dairy, cooked food, onion and citrus in large quantities (small amounts are tolerated but large quantities repel worms), or oily foods.
Capacity: a standard two-tray worm bin processes roughly 2-3 kg of scraps per week. If the household generates more than this, add a second tray or manage some waste via another route.

fills the gap a worm bin cannot: cooked food, meat, fish, and dairy. A sealed bucket with an airtight lid accepts all food waste including the items that cannot go to a worm bin. A tablespoon of bokashi bran is added after each layer. The bucket is kept sealed between additions and is full in approximately two to four weeks for a typical household.

When full, the fermented material must be buried or added to a compost heap; it is not finished compost and should not be applied directly to plant roots or containers. Households without any garden can arrange to bury the pre-compost in a park, an allotment, or a community garden. Some councils have collection schemes for bokashi pre-compost.

Under the Simpler Recycling regulations, all English local authorities must provide separate food waste collection from 2027. Many already do. A small caddy sits in the kitchen and is emptied weekly into the kerbside collection. This is the no-effort baseline for households that cannot maintain a worm bin or bokashi system. Check the local authority website to confirm what is accepted; most schemes accept cooked and raw food, meat, and dairy.

Community composting sites, often run by local allotment associations or community gardens, accept raw fruit and vegetable waste from local households. The output is shared among members or used on the site. For a block of flats with any outdoor space, a single shared bin managed collectively is also an option.
